udaism,—these heresies, amid all their compleity and diversity, t. ippolytus   reduces to one
common ground of censure—antagonism to oly cripture. eresy, thus branded, he leaves to
wither under the condemnatory sentence of the hurch.

they bind him with an oath neither to divulge (the mysteries), nor to hold communication with any
person whatsoever, unless he first undergo similar subjection, though, when the doctrine has been
simply delivered (to any one), there was no longer any need of an oath. or he who was content to
submit to the necessary purgation,   and so receive the perfect mysteries of these men, by the very
act itself, as well as in reference to his own conscience, will feel himself sufficiently under an
obligation not to divulge to others for if he once disclose wickedness of this description to any
man, he would neither be reckoned among men, nor be deemed worthy to behold the light, since
not even irrational animals   would attempt such an enormity, as we shall eplain when we come
to treat of such topics.
ince, however, reason compels us to plunge   into the very depth of narrative, we conceive
we should not be silent, but, epounding the tenets of the several schools with minuteness, we shall
evince reserve in nothing. ow it seems epedient, even at the epense of a more protracted
investigation, not to shrink from labour for we shall leave behind us no trifling auiliary to human
life against the recurrence of error, when all are made to behold, in an obvious light, the clandestine
rites of these men, and the secret orgies which, retaining under their management, they deliver to
the initiated only. ut none will refute these, save the oly pirit bequeathed unto the hurch,
which the postles, having in the first instance received, have transmitted to those who have rightly
believed. ut we, as being their successors, and as participators in this grace, high-priesthood, and
office of teaching,   as well as being reputed guardians of the hurch, must not be found deficient
in vigilance,   or disposed to suppress correct doctrine.   ot even, however, labouring with every
energy of body and soul, do we tire in our attempt adequately to render our ivine enefactor a

remiss in discharging the trust committed to us, but careful to complete the measure of our particular
opportunity, and to impart to all without grudging whatever the oly host supplies, not only
bringing to light,   by means of our refutation, matters foreign (to our subject), but also whatsoever

things the truth has received by the grace of the ather,   and ministered to men. hese also,
illustrating by argument and creating testimony   by letters, we shall unabashed proclaim.
n order, then, as we have already stated, that we may prove them atheists, both in opinion and
their mode (of treating a question) and in fact, and (in order to show) whence it is that their attempted
theories have accrued unto them, and that they have endeavoured to establish their tenets, taking
nothing from the holy criptures—nor is it from preserving the succession of any saint that they
have hurried headlong into these opinions—but that their doctrines have derived their origin   from
the wisdom of the reeks, from the conclusions of those who have formed systems of philosophy,
and from would-be mysteries, and the vagaries of astrologers,—it seems, then, advisable, in the
first instance, by eplaining the opinions advanced by the philosophers of the reeks, to satisfy
our readers that such are of greater antiquity than these (heresies), and more deserving of reverence
in reference to their views respecting the divinity in the net place, to compare each heresy with
the system of each speculator, so as to show that the earliest champion of the heresy availing
himself   of these attempted theories, has turned them to advantage by appropriating their principles,
 
 and, impelled from these into worse, has constructed his own doctrine. he undertaking admittedly
is full of labour, and (is one) requiring etended research. e shall not, however, be wanting in
eertion for afterwards it will be a source of joy, just like an athlete obtaining with much toil the
crown, or a merchant after a huge swell of sea compassing gain, or a husbandman after sweat of
brow enjoying the fruits, or a prophet after reproaches and insults seeing his predictions turning
out true. n the commencement, therefore, we shall declare who first, among the reeks, pointed
out (the principles of) natural philosophy. or from these especially have they furtively taken their
views who have first propounded these heresies,   as we shall subsequently prove when we come
to compare them one with another.   ssigning to each of those who take the lead among philosophers
their own peculiar tenets, we shall publicly ehibit these heresiarchs as naked and unseemly.

t is said that hales of iletus, one of the seven   wise men, first attempted to frame a system
of natural philosophy. his person said that some such thing as water is the generative principle of
the universe, and its end—for that out of this, solidified and again dissolved, all things consist,
and that all things are supported on it from which also arise both earthquakes and changes of the
winds and atmospheric movements,   and that all things are both produced   and are in a state of
flu corresponding with the nature of the primary author of generation—and that the eity   is
that which has neither beginning nor end. his person, having been occupied with an hypothesis
and investigation concerning the stars, became the earliest author to the reeks of this kind of
learning. nd he, looking towards heaven, alleging that he was carefully eamining supernal objects,
fell into a well and a certain maid, by name hratta, remarked of him derisively, that while intent
on beholding things in heaven, he did not know   what was at his feet. nd he lived about the time
of rœsus.

have ordered his followers not to eat beans, because that aratas said that, at the origin and concretion
of all things, when the earth was still undergoing its process of solidification,   and that of
putrefaction had set in, the bean was produced.   nd of this he mentions the following indication,
that if any one, after having chewed a bean without the husk, places it opposite the sun for a certain
period,—for this immediately will aid in the result,—it yields the smell of human seed. nd he
mentions also another clearer instance to be this if, when the bean is blossoming, we take the bean
and its flower, and deposit them in a jar, smear this over, and bury it in the ground, and after a few
days uncover it, we shall see it wearing the appearance, first of a woman’s   pudendum , and after
this, when closely eamined, of the head of a child growing in along with it. his person, being
burned along with his disciples in roton, a town of taly, perished. nd this was a habit with him,
whenever one repaired to him with a view of becoming his follower, (the candidate disciple was
compelled) to sell his possessions, and lodge the money sealed with ythagoras, and he continued
 
 in silence to undergo instruction, sometimes for three, but sometimes for five years. nd again, on
being released, he was permitted to associate with the rest, and remained as a disciple, and took
his meals along with them if otherwise, however, he received back his property, and was rejected.
hese persons, then, were styled soteric ythagoreans, whereas the rest, ythagoristæ.
mong his followers, however, who escaped the conflagration were ysis and rchippus, and
the servant of ythagoras, amolis,   who also is said to have taught the eltic ruids to cultivate
the philosophy of ythagoras. nd they assert that ythagoras learned from the gyptians his
system of numbers and measures and being struck by the plausible, fanciful, and not easily revealed
wisdom of the priests, he himself likewise, in imitation of them, enjoined silence, and made his
disciples lead a solitary life in underground chapels.

hapter .—tory of psethus the ibyan.
psethus   the ibyan inordinately longed to become a god but when, after repeated intrigues,
he altogether failed to accomplish his desire, he nevertheless wished to appear to have become   a
god  and he did at all events appear, as time wore on, to have in reality become a god. or the

they were yielding credence to a voice that came down from above, from heaven. or, collecting
into one and the same cage a great number of birds,—parrots,—he shut them up. ow there are
very many parrots throughout ibya, and very distinctly these imitate the human voice. his man,
having for a time nourished the birds, was in the habit of teaching them to say, “psethus is a god.”
fter, however, the birds had practised this for a long period, and were accustomed to the utterance
of that which he thought, when said, would make it supposed that psethus was a god, then, opening
the habitation (of the birds), he let forth the parrots, each in a different direction. hile the birds,
however, were on the wing, their sound went out into all ibya, and the epressions of these reached
as far as the ellenic country. nd thus the ibyans, being astonished at the voice of the birds, and
not perceiving the knavery perpetrated by psethus, held psethus to be a god. ome one, however,
of the reeks, by accurate eamination, perceiving the trick of the supposed god, by means of those
same parrots not only refutes, but also utterly destroys, that boastful and tiresome fellow. ow the
reek, by confining many of the parrots, taught them anew to say, “psethus, having caged us,
compelled us to say, psethus is a god.” ut having heard of the recantation of the parrots, the
ibyans, coming together, all unanimously decided on burning psethus.
